data.title: Global stablecoin surge highlights overlooked risks in crypto finance
data.description:
In May 2023, Australian economist Paul Sheard published a book, The Power of Money, explaining how governments and banks create money. Things have moved on since then in the world of cryptocurrency, and it is the little understood international politics of money that need clearing up now.
Public interest continues to centre upon instruments such as bitcoin and its cryptocurrency imitators, whose huge price swings attract headlines. These are not currencies, however, but “risk assets” as Sheard noted during a recent event at the Foreign Correspondents’ Club of Japan in Tokyo.
The focus should be upon developments in the United States, China, Japan and Europe, where the battle for currency domination is accelerating via the issue of stablecoins – a private-sector cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to another asset, typically a fiat currency – and also central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Both innovations could influence the global balance of currency power as well as domestic economic issues.
Pilot programmes in the use of stablecoins are spreading across the Asia-Pacific. Interest is also growing in CBDCs, with the region becoming a centre for digital payments innovation, Tobias Adrian, director of the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF) monetary and capital markets department, said during a seminar in Tokyo last week.
The politics of cryptocurrencies are intriguing. Bitcoin and other such instruments came into the world with their innovators wishing to bypass the existing system of sovereign currencies because they distrusted governments and central banks. By using blockchain technology to enable fast and cheap financial transactions instead of bank-to-bank exchanges, the belief was that such obstacles could be overcome.
Instead, holders of bitcoin and similar digital assets found that the value of these instruments was wildly volatile, even though their restricted issuance seemed to promise stability. Stablecoins linked to collateral holdings of US dollars and other sovereign securities seemed to provide an answer, even if they shifted control of monetary policy from official authorities to corporate and financial entities.


The fact that stablecoin holdings could be moved more or less freely across national borders alarmed governments and central banks. They responded by devising CBDCs, which are direct liabilities of the issuing central bank and therefore safe, in theory. The various cryptocurrency instruments are now in a competition with the legacy currency system.
The politics around digital currencies have extended into the long-running battle for supremacy between the world’s major currencies. Through legislation and his family’s business activities, US President Donald Trump is promoting the development of US dollar-backed stablecoins to ensure the continued dominance of the US currency. Nine leading European banks have joined forces to launch a euro-denominated stablecoin, which, according to ING, “aims to become a trusted European payment standard in the digital ecosystem”.
Perhaps with an eye towards preventing premature internationalisation of the yuan, the Chinese government has taken a cautious stance on stablecoin issuance while observing Hong Kong’s experiments in this arena. Meanwhile, Japan is taking a more active approach as it views the global spread of the yen as something that should be encouraged.
Japan’s Financial Services Agency announced on November 7 that it would support a pilot project by three major banks – Mizuho, Mitsubishi UFJ and Sumitomo Mitsui – to jointly issue stablecoins. This came shortly after Japanese tech start-up company JPYC launched the world’s first yen-backed stablecoin.


Tommaso Mancini-Griffoli, assistant director of the IMF’s monetary and capital markets department, said during a presentation at the Tokyo seminar that 91 per cent of 93 central banks surveyed last year indicated they were exploring CBDCs. An April 2024 report from a meeting between the Bank of Japan and multiple government ministries noted that “many economies have been studying retail CBDC (general-purpose CBDC) for individual and business users while a few, including the Bahamas, have officially introduced it”.
Interest in stablecoins is surging around the world amid their promise of faster, cheaper payments and a wave of financial innovation. At the same time, however, their spread is accelerating concerns about their effects on global financial systems. In a commentary for Project Syndicate, Angus Armstrong, chief economic adviser at Lloyds Banking Group, and Dennis J. Snower, president emeritus of the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, warn that “stablecoins don’t just disrupt monetary policy; they also reshape fiscal dynamics. Every dollar of stablecoin issuance translates into more demand for government debt. As automatic buyers of Treasury bills, stablecoin issuers are creating a captive market that suppresses yields”.
Meanwhile, the European Systemic Risk Board noted in a report last month that “forceful US policy measures” were supporting the mainstreaming of cryptocurrency assets, including stablecoins. “Amid a fragmented global regulatory landscape, US crypto policies seek to strengthen the dollar’s dominance by promoting the development and adoption of USD-denominated stablecoins worldwide,” the report said.


The report expressed concern about the growing links between the cryptocurrency and financial sectors, underscoring “the urgent need to introduce safeguards against financial stability risks through enhanced supervisory measures, legislative reforms and closer international cooperation”. It singled out stablecoins’ “spillover risks due to their rapid growth, their steadily increasing ties to traditional finance through backing assets and the risks associated with a potential growing role in payment systems”.
The wild swings in value and potential risks to global financial stability have not dampened enthusiasm for cryptocurrency and other digital assets. As such, greater public education on these systems and their risks is necessary before they fully go global.

data.title: China set to benefit as South Africa looks to diversify away from US
data.description:
South Africa is looking to new export markets in Asia – particularly China – and Europe to diversify away from the United States, as its relations with Washington rapidly deteriorate.
America boycotted last weekend’s Group of 20 summit in Johannesburg after Washington accused Pretoria of persecuting Afrikaner farmers, labelling the situation “white genocide” – a claim the South African government strongly denies.
On Wednesday the situation escalated, with US President Donald Trump saying Pretoria would not be invited to next year’s G20 talks in Miami. South Africa called the move “regrettable” and based on “misinformation and distortions” and said it had a right to attend as a sovereign, founding member of the G20.
It comes two months after a US trade initiative with Africa – the African Growth and Opportunity Act – expired, ending duty-free access for many South African exports.
In addition, a 30 per cent tariff imposed on South African exports by the US has meanwhile severely hurt farms, vehicle manufacturers and car parts makers, as well as mining companies.

South Africa is responding by pursuing new markets in Asia and Europe to diversify exports and attract investment.
According to analysts, the pivot can be seen in South Africa’s diplomatic activities in countries like China, Vietnam and Indonesia, as well as in the European Union, with efforts under way to secure investment and new market access for South African goods, particularly in manufacturing and agriculture.
The analysts said China was emerging as the main beneficiary as it stood to deepen economic and political ties with South Africa, while the US was at risk of ceding influence across the African continent.
Tim Zajontz, interim professor in International Relations at the University of Freiburg, said the South African government was seeking to further diversify its political and economic ties.
Pretoria aimed to redirect some of its exports – especially car parts, other manufactured goods and agricultural produce – to other destinations through a combination of subsidies, trade facilitation measures and economic diplomacy, according to Zajontz.
He said the efforts were being directed towards Europe, the Gulf states and various Asian and Latin American countries.
“The estrangement from the US will further intensify economic and political cooperation between South Africa and China,” Zajontz added.
To cultivate new economic allies in Asia, a South African delegation has visited China, Vietnam and Indonesia. President Cyril Ramaphosa also took part in the EU’s Global Gateway Forum in Brussels in October and recently visited Ireland and Switzerland in pursuit of investors and new markets.
Chrispin Phiri, spokesman for South Africa’s international relations minister, confirmed the nation was intensifying efforts to diversify its markets.
He acknowledged that the US was a substantial market for South Africa but said it was “determined to pursue economic diplomacy in earnest” in a bid to ramp up trade with other places.
One of those is China. During a meeting with South African leaders on the sidelines of the G20 summit, Chinese Premier Li Qiang said Beijing was willing to align development strategies, accelerate zero-tariff measures, and deepen cooperation in sectors like mining, infrastructure, cars and new energy.
He also pledged to deepen political mutual trust, firmly support South Africa’s core interests, and strengthen cooperation within platforms like Brics and the G20.

Zajontz, who is also a research fellow with the Centre for International and Comparative Politics at Stellenbosch University, said that in contrast with the US, China was opening its vast market to African goods and services.
“We are also going to see additional Chinese investments in South Africa’s automotive industry as well as in transport, energy and digital infrastructure,” he said.
Meanwhile, the Clean Trade and Investment Partnership signed between South Africa and Europe secures new market access and significant investment for South Africa’s green economy transition. The deal focuses on promoting battery manufacturing and facilitating South Africa’s export of electric vehicles to the EU market.
South Africa’s strained US relations are part of a broader pattern. Ties with Nigeria were similarly frayed after Trump threatened military action over what he claimed was the government’s failure to prevent widespread killings of Christians – allegations Abuja rejected.
According to John Calabrese, a non-resident senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, Washington’s absence from the G20 allowed Beijing to project itself as a reliable partner, strengthen economic ties and reinforce its narrative as Africa’s “go-to ally”.
He said the move reinforced incentives for African nations to lean into Brics and Chinese investment and signalled that US support was neither automatic nor guaranteed.
Calabrese said the move was shortsighted and risked the erosion of American influence while reinforcing China’s foothold in Africa.
China has gained influence by providing needed infrastructure across the continent, according to Professor John Kirton, director of the G20 Research Group at the University of Toronto.
“[For supply chains] you really need ports and railroads – and Africa needs a lot more of that still,” he added.

data.title: In ‘Schrodinger’s Pakistan’, the young feel sidelined by their own state
data.description:
In Pakistan, a nation where power often seems to serve itself above all else, the latest constitutional overhaul has been received with a weary sense of resignation.
Pushed through parliament on November 13 with barely a murmur of opposition, the 27th amendment to the country’s 1973 constitution made two major changes: splitting the Supreme Court into separate appellate and constitutional benches, and upgrading the army chief of staff to overall commander of Pakistan’s armed forces.
Viewed in isolation, both moves have plausible justifications. Pakistan’s higher judiciary has been swamped by cases for decades – a backlog compounded by activist judges who, since the restoration of democracy in 2008, have devoted extraordinary time to politically charged hearings.
Meanwhile, the government had argued it needed a more integrated command structure to meet the demands of modern warfare following a brief but deadly aerial conflict with India in May, replacing the largely ceremonial chairman of the joint chiefs with a hands-on chief of defence forces.

Seen against Pakistan’s longer political history, however, the amendments are more calculated power grabs than practical governance changes.
With its commanding parliamentary majority, Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s multiparty coalition has seized an opportunity to neutralise an unpredictable Supreme Court whose populist, partisan interventions have repeatedly destabilised elected governments, while simultaneously appeasing the armed forces’ appetite for control.
Pakistan’s top judges have repeatedly thrown their weight into the political arena in recent years, toppling prime ministers and acting as bulwarks for favoured leaders. Both Yusuf Raza Gilani in 2012 and Nawaz Sharif in 2017 were removed from office on dubious legal grounds, while a former chief justice was accused of giving special treatment to Imran Khan, who is currently serving multiple prison sentences including 14 years for corruption.

By bifurcating the Supreme Court, Sharif’s coalition is also widely seen as doing the military’s bidding. Pakistan’s generals have made little secret of their desire to keep Khan behind bars until he falls into line, as much of the country’s political class already has.
True to his combative persona, Khan has refused to yield, apparently banking on a mix of mass popularity and patience to eventually topple what critics call Pakistan’s “hybrid democracy” – a system in which elected leaders and the military govern in tandem, but with the generals ultimately retaining veto power.
‘Schrodinger’s Pakistan’
Across Pakistan, the amendments have landed with a thud. Seven Pakistanis interviewed by This Week In Asia – men and women aged 22 to 51 from the capital Islamabad, the eastern metropolis of Lahore and farming communities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Punjab – expressed varying degrees of disillusionment.
“There seems to be a quiet power struggle between the parliament, the military and the judiciary,” said Ajalan Ali, 24, an Islamabad resident who recently graduated from a Turkish university with a degree in international relations and political science. “Rather than coexisting, each entity seems to be struggling to expand its control over Pakistan.”

For Marukh Khalid, a 22-year-old undergraduate at Lahore University of Management Sciences, the problem is broader than any single amendment. “All institutions in Pakistan have become increasingly self-serving and have failed to do much of anything for the common people,” she said.
“Each institution ultimately tries to be the most powerful of the powerful, while the rest of the country is forced to watch, and we see that with the 27th amendment.”
From her vantage point at Lahore’s Forman Christian College University, where she manages research projects and public relations, 30-year-old Ayesha Siddiqua sees in the reforms an unvarnished display of power.
“These institutions have now openly shown the people of Pakistan how things run and who runs them,” she said, noting a “drastic change” since Khan was toppled after the military withdrew its support.
“Instead of saving face, they [the generals] have made themselves more powerful by forcing their hand on politicians, the judiciary and parliament, giving them no other choice but to comply with them.”

In her view, that might bring a perverse upside. The arrangement “might be good to some extent since it may lead to stability in the government at least,” she said.
For Aneesah Amer Shah, the 27th amendment is not a cure for institutional decay but its symptom. All three branches of the state – the executive, the legislature and the judiciary – “have failed to perform their constitutional duties and the 27th amendment is a clear reflection of that failure”, said the 24-year-old architect from Swat.
From her small Basmati rice farm along the banks of the River Chenab, 51-year-old Rehana Afzal has reached a similar conclusion. “Every institution is showing a decline in its performance,” she said. “Most are just supporting the military’s vision for the country.”
Others see the hybrid system, in which civilian and military leaders share power uneasily, as an unpleasant but necessary compromise. “For the time being, hybrid democracy is reality,” said Syed Taimur Jan, a member of Khan’s Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf party from the mountainous district of Dir, bordering Afghanistan. “But it won’t sustain for long.”

Self-described “realist” Nibaat Hassan Syed, 24, who is pursuing a master’s degree in political science and whose brother is training to be an army officer, stressed that democracy in Pakistan had “always existed alongside the state institutions that play a stabilising role”.
“Many people feel that, given Pakistan’s unique security concerns, a mixed model where civilian leadership and strong institutions work together provides continuity and order,” she said.
Rather than labelling it hybrid, Syed prefers to frame Pakistan’s system as a “pragmatic” arrangement designed “to keep the country functioning during political instability”.
Those interviewed by This Week in Asia were just as divided about the country’s trajectory in the years ahead.
Siddiqua in Lahore is cautiously hopeful. She expected “things to get well, [though] there might not be an absolute democracy any time soon in Pakistan”. If the hybrid order delivered basic governance, “I am OK with it”, she said.

Khalid, the undergraduate student, fears the opposite. To her, “this is just the beginning of the end of democracy in Pakistan”.
“Critical issues will be disregarded, and power and wealth will remain in the hands of the few,” she warned, predicting the country was “going to become more unsafe than it already is, especially for anyone with an opinion”.
Ali, the recent graduate in Islamabad, was more upbeat, arguing that Pakistan has a knack for defying expectations – for better and for worse.
“This is Schrodinger’s Pakistan,” he quipped – in a nod to the famous thought experiment in which a cat in a box is considered both dead and alive until observed – noting that just a year ago “no one would believe” that by 2025, Pakistan would see its leaders welcomed at the White House, sign a mutual defence pact with Saudi Arabia, shoot down attacking Indian warplanes and begin purging Afghanistan-based terrorist groups from its territory.
Looking to the Pakistan Stock Exchange for inspiration, Ali said, “I see Pakistan going a bit down before rising up – akin to a J-curve”.

data.title: Bangladesh ex-PM Khaleda Zia in ‘very critical’ condition
data.description:
Former Bangladesh prime minister Khaleda Zia’s health deteriorated since being admitted to hospital, prompting her family and party members to urge citizens to pray for her speedy recovery on Saturday.
Zia, 80, was admitted to hospital on November 23 with symptoms of a lung infection and was currently undergoing treatment in the intensive care unit, members of her party said.
Mirza Fakhrul Alamgir, from Zia’s Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP), told reporters late Friday that “doctors have told us that her condition is very critical”.
Several senior BNP leaders and anxious supporters visited the hospital on Saturday to receive updates on her condition.
English-language newspaper, The Daily Star, said Zia had “heart problems, liver and kidney issues, diabetes, lung problems, arthritis and eye-related illnesses”.
She had a permanent pacemaker and previously underwent stenting for her heart, the publication reported.
Zia’s eldest son, Tarique Rahman, who has been based in London since 2008, requested the people of Bangladesh pray for his mother’s recovery in a social media most on Saturday.

“We express our heartfelt thanks and gratitude for all your prayers and love for the highly respected Begum Khaleda Zia,” Rahman, 60, said.
Rahman said he was unable to return to Bangladesh because of circumstances that were beyond his control.
“I share the same deep longing as any child to have my mother’s touch in moments of crisis. However, the decision to return home is neither straightforward nor mine alone to make,” he said.
Zia, who served three terms as prime minister, was jailed for corruption in 2018 under Sheikh Hasina’s government, which also barred her from travelling abroad for medical treatment.
She was released last year, shortly after Hasina’s ousting.
Interim leader Muhammad Yunus also issued a statement.
“During this transitional period to democracy, Khaleda Zia is a source of utmost inspiration for the nation. Her recovery is very important for the country,” he said on Friday night.
Despite her ill health, Zia has vowed to campaign in elections expected in February 2026, in which BNP is widely seen as a front runner.

data.title: Japan security guard’s special bond with cat moves many; museum honours feline’s memory
data.description:
A special bond that was formed between a museum security guard and a black cat that died last year continues to move countless people online.
The feline called Ken-chan visited the Onomichi City Museum in Japan’s Hiroshima prefecture for the first time in 2017, during a special exhibition themed on cats.
He tried to enter the museum but was gently stopped by the guard, Sadao Umayahara.

Instead of repelling Ken-chan, Umayahara patiently bent down to block his way. The cat also seemed to enjoy playing with Umayahara.
Museum staff captured their interaction, and photographs of it attracted attention online.
As a fixed-term guard for the museum, Umayahara only worked there four times a year during special exhibitions.
Museum staff noticed that Ken-chan visited the museum mostly when Umayahara was on duty.

Every time the feline turned up, staff would have their cameras at the ready to capture the pair’s interaction.
The such meeting between the two took place on April 18, last year.
Ken-chan managed to make his way inside, but stopped by himself after going a few metres.
Umayahara then picked him up and took him to the restaurant area, ultimately winning their battle of wits.

An employee who had watched their interactions for seven years said the animal did not want to go to the museum, but only wanted Umayahara to play with him.
Ken-chan stopped visiting the museum in July last year, and rarely went outside due to illness, according to his owner.
Despite extensive treatment, the friendly feline died in September.
The museum announced his death on behalf of his owner on November 11, the day when Ken-chan was adopted by his owner.
Umayahara, who had only kept a dog as a pet, said Ken-chan was the only cat that was close to him.
He said he was grateful to his feline friend: “I still feel like he is coming from around that corner.”

The museum has released a 2026 calendar in memory of Ken-chan, filling it with photographs of sweet moments with Umayahara.
The calendar ends with a picture of Ken-chan standing in front of the restaurant wearing a bow tie.
Staff who selected the photo said they did so to convey that he was finally spending his time happily in his beloved home.
The museum will continue to share Ken-chan’s healthy and happy moments in future.
“Ken-chan’s soul can now enter the museum freely,” said one online observer.
“I love how the museum staff treated a feline so attentively,” said another.

data.title: Catch Spencer Sweeney’s Hong Kong exhibition, US artist who paints like he’s making jazz
data.description:
For American painter Spencer Sweeney, the artistic process is less of a linear path and more of an improvised musical composition, filled with subconscious rhythm and unpredictable bursts of colour.
This is perhaps unsurprising given that the 52-year-old grew up in a family of musicians and artists, and works as a painter and a DJ in New York, his home since graduating from the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts in 1997.
At his first solo exhibition in Hong Kong, Sweeney explains to the Post that his art practice goes beyond the portraits seen in the gallery – it is an immersive and multisensory process related to his other creative outlets, such as the “noise art” group he set up in 1997 with fellow musician-artist Lizzi Bougatsos and the legendary nightclub Santos Party House (2008-2016) that he co-founded in Manhattan.
“I am always thinking about how one invents their own ways of depicting and representing things,” he says.

The oil paintings showing at Hong Kong’s Gagosian art gallery also reflect more traditional influences. Citing inspiration from Edouard Manet, J.M.W. Turner, Beauford Delaney and Henri Matisse, Sweeney says he has always been drawn to artists “who can embody both realms of abstract expression and presentation simultaneously”.
The woman in a new work titled Kama on Halloween (2025), which is composed of rapid, bold brushstrokes, is fairly representational, with her spookily made-up face featuring additional painted eyes in a cool bluish grey palette, in contrast to the bright red top she is wearing.
The girl in Girl at Fete (2025) and the figure in Madame Captain (2025) are more abstract, their features reduced to geometric forms and free-flowing lines.


Sweeney says the subconscious guides his progress, and he improvises like a jazz musician, waiting for the figures on canvas to “start evolving and personalities to emerge”, rather than fixating on meticulous planning and meaning-seeking.
And, just as jazz is often created in a room full of people, he much prefers to work in an “open studio”.
In 2017, he started Headz, a weekly salon held in New York that he co-founded with Swiss artist Urs Fischer and New Zealand musician Brendan Dugan. Experimental jazz musicians would play while Sweeney and others painted on communal tables. Everyone was welcome, and the solitary practice of a studio artist turned into something more community-oriented.
“The mindset and awareness that I have to conjure to be able to improvise musically is a very similar state to that which has to be summoned to paint … there’s similarities in space, activity, rhythm, tone, colour and texture.”

When Sweeney was planning for the Hong Kong exhibition, he had hoped to explore these philosophies and to create his new paintings during his residency in Thailand, organised by the Bangkok Kunsthalle, a contemporary art space that opened in the Thai capital in 2024.
Founded by Thai philanthropist Marisa Chearavanont and led by former Hauser & Wirth director Stefano Rabolli Pansera, the Bangkok Kunsthalle has given second life to an abandoned printing house and now stands as a visionary brutalist complex.
As an artist-in-residence hosted by the Kunsthalle from September to December 2025, Sweeney wanted to make his studio in Bangkok an immersive multimedia environment with performance stages.
“I’m not a performance painter,” he emphasises, but he is passionate about creating spaces where music and paintings feed off each other’s creative energy, and one thing cannot work without the other.

His plans were disturbed, however, thanks to unforeseen construction issues at the Kunsthalle, and he painted out of a studio in Chiang Mai rather than Bangkok in the end. But with a slight pivot, Sweeney was still able to create an installation in one of the galleries at the Bangkok Kunsthalle.
With the help of local artisans specialising in bamboo structures and expert sound technicians, Sweeney also created a DJ booth where artists and visitors could experience listening and screening sessions in the Kunsthalle.
His plans for the open studio space in Bangkok, however, will probably have to wait until March when he returns to programme a coming Kunsthalle gala.
Sweeney was last in Thailand in 2013. A lot has changed since, he notices. “The artistic community has grown and fortified, and has really become a very robust and vital movement.”
Currently, he is looking forward to cultural cross-pollination via the Hong Kong exhibition, where all of the paintings were completed in Chiang Mai.
“It’s interesting to see people relate to your work in different cultures, different scenarios,” he says, eager to see how a Hong Kong audience will react to his work.
For Sweeney, it is an especially interesting time for art in Asia, and he sees it as the next pioneer for contemporary art.
“People can feel it – and I can see it happening.”
“Paint”, Gagosian, 7/F, Pedder Building, 12 Pedder Street, Central. Tel: 2151 0555. From November 19 until December 23.

data.title: ‘Stable and resilient’: China gives disputed Scarborough Shoal a clean bill of health
data.description:
Coral reefs at the disputed Scarborough Shoal in the South China Sea are healthy and resilient to climate change, but growing thermal stress poses a potential bleaching risk, according to a Chinese environmental assessment.
The report rated the atoll’s maritime environment as excellent, with no signs of cyanide in seawater, sediment or fish samples.
Scarborough Shoal, known as Huangyan Island in China and Panatag Shoal in the Philippines, is claimed by both countries, and Manila has accused Chinese crews of using cyanide to catch fish in the area.




The report was released on Friday and was conducted by various agencies led by the Chinese Ministry of Ecology and Environment.
China has held de facto control over Scarborough Shoal since a naval stand-off in 2012 and has steadily increased its presence in the area in the years since.
In September, Beijing approved a plan to designate the shoal as a nature reserve, prompting a strong protest from Manila, which said that it “has the exclusive authority to establish environmental protection areas over its territory and relevant maritime zones”.
In its report on Friday, the Chinese ministry led off with the sovereignty issue.
“Huangyan Island, also known as Scarborough Shoal, is part of the Zhongsha Islands and is an inherent territory of our country,” it said in its first line.
“Huangyan Island’s environment is diverse, stable and sustainable, providing essential habitats for marine life and serving as an important indicator of global climate change and environmental evolution.”


The report was based on a survey conducted in August by researchers from the ministry, two institutions in the southern island province of Hainan, and Guangxi University.
The team collected 1,740 samples and carried out underwater photography and drone surveys to assess the quality of seawater, sediment and fauna. They also assessed the state of marine debris, coral reefs and seagrass beds.
Expanding on findings from the previous year, they found that “the environmental quality of the Huangyan Island maritime area remains excellent”.
According to the report, pollutants such as heavy metals and petroleum hydrocarbons were below standard limits in fish samples.
They also said the coral reef ecosystem was healthy and stable, documenting 134 species of reef-building corals – 25 more than in 2024 – with live coral covering about 30 per cent of the reefs.
All the identified reef-building coral species are listed as protected in China and are on the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s red list of threatened species. Some are designated as endangered or vulnerable on the red list, according to the report.


With rich biodiversity, a slight increase in live coral coverage compared to last year and no signs of coral bleaching or disease among the reef-building corals, the team said the ecosystem showed strong resilience and adaptability to global climate change.
“However, as the extent of climate change intensifies, the increasing thermal stress presents a potential risk of coral bleaching that cannot be overlooked,” they said.

data.title: Hotelier races to shelter Hong Kong fire victims using Grenfell disaster lessons
data.description:
A hotelier is drawing on her experience sheltering survivors of London’s Grenfell Tower disaster to spearhead a relief operation at Hong Kong’s former Covid-19 isolation centre as she races to source 700 beds for families displaced by the Wang Fuk Court fire.
Winnie Chiu Wing-kwan, the president and executive director of Dorsett Hospitality International, has taken personal charge of logistics at Runway 1331 in Kai Tak, which has opened 500 guest rooms to accommodate up to 2,000 affected residents.
“I worked until 4am for two consecutive days … the most urgent need is to make rooms habitable immediately for those in need, so we need mattresses and bed frames,” Chiu said.
With about 300 rooms already occupied, the operator is urgently seeking 700 ready-made single bed sets to maximise space, noting that traumatised families are requesting to share rooms for emotional support despite the compact unit sizes.
“Even if the units are small, they want four people in one room because … they just want to be together with each other,” Chiu said.
She said previously ordered furniture shipments from mainland China were still being assembled.

Chiu is applying lessons learned from 2017, when she opened the Dorsett Shepherds Bush to survivors of the Grenfell Tower blaze, providing shelter for up to a year. The hotel’s rooms with double beds currently command rates of around HK$2,325 (US$300) per night next Thursday on Booking.com.
About 70 out of 317 rooms of the hotel, which is about a 23-minute drive from London’s Heathrow Airport, were occupied by the victims, she added.
“Continuing to help will be quite a long journey … at least for the next six months,” she said.
With basic single frames and mattresses retailing at least HK$499 and HK$499.90 each on IKEA’s website, the drive to furnish the Kai Tak units represents a significant financial undertaking of at least HK$699,230 for 700 sets, excluding logistical and labour costs.
The Runway 1331 operator has established a 24-hour hotline at 5635 7131 for donors, emphasising that while general supplies have poured in, the facility specifically requires sleeping furniture to increase capacity.
“Currently, we urgently require bed frames to accommodate more affected residents,” the organisation said in a statement.
She said donors could call the hotline in advance to check the latest supplies needed, adding she was considering deploying Instagram and a website to display updated requirements.

Given that donors had already sent many clothes, requiring time to process, Chiu called for a platform to match donations with actual needs.
The relief effort will soon transition to a second phase focused on personalised aid, such as replacing school uniforms and textbooks for students who escaped with nothing but the clothes on their backs.
Chiu described the initiative as part of her and her company’s “invest in positive impact” philosophy, emphasising that the “Social” aspect of ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance) is often the hardest to measure but the most vital.
“I truly believe in taking from society and using it for society … especially the ‘S’ part of ESG,” she said.
“Actually, the reason I like Hong Kong so much is that Hong Kong is much warmer,” Chiu said. “Supplies provided in Hong Kong are definitely the most abundant and well-organised; [supplies provided in the UK] are not as impressive.”
The fire at Wang Fuk Court, a private residential estate, killed at least 128 people, including a firefighter, and injured 79, including 12 firefighters.
The blaze ignited at Wang Cheong House on Wednesday and quickly spread to seven other buildings in the estate, quickly spiralling into Hong Kong’s worst conflagration in decades.
Named after the old airport’s single airstrip, designated Runway 1331, the project has been developed by Glorise Tourism and Culture, a joint venture between the central government-owned China Tourism Group and entrepreneur Winnie Chiu.
The government is coordinating with the information technology sector to construct an online platform for residents and groups to register donations that will be distributed to victims as needed.
The Home and Youth Affairs Bureau said the public can register via WhatsApp at 9213 2388 to facilitate government matching donations with required supplies.

data.title: ‘Sad but glad to help’: Hong Kong fire teaches children lesson in empathy
data.description:
Beneath the podium at Kwong Fuk Estate in Tai Po, between rows of relief booths and supply tables stacked with bottled water and meal boxes, a group of children took a break from their volunteering duties.
But they did not rest for long. As soon as they finished eating, they darted through the crowds to reach residents, their arms full of bottles of hot water, ginger tea and bags of essentials, offering warmth to anyone who needed it on a cold Friday night.
Under their parents’ guidance, six children spent Thursday and Friday evenings, from 6pm to 10pm, checking stock and calling out to residents about the available supplies.
For 10-year-old Leif Ng, a Primary Five student at NTWJWA Christian Remembrance of Grace Primary School, the fire prompted him to join the community effort.
“I felt a bit sad. We saw so many people hurt and dead,” said the Tai Po resident. Still, he said he was glad that he and his 12-year-old brother, Liam, could help in some way.
“I did not expect so many people would come to help,” he added, recalling how he handed out meal boxes and heat packs to elderly residents.

His schoolmate, Primary Six student Rainbow Lam, said she had been at Kwong Fuk Estate with her father and her 10-year-old sister, Summer, for two nights in a row.
“I felt both happy and sad,” the 12-year-old said. “Happy because we could help others, but sad because when I saw Wang Fuk Court, the buildings looked abandoned after the fire.”
Despite mountains of donated supplies piling up around them, the sisters said the same thing: “The residents need homes; they need somewhere safe to stay.”
The group plans to continue volunteering until Sunday.
Among them were two more 11-year-olds from the same primary school, Summer Chan and Elena Ho, whose tasks included filling water bottles and calling out to passing residents.
“We were distributing hot water today,” Summer Chan said. “Some elderly people do not need them, but they would come up to us and compliment us.”
For Sing Lam, the father of Rainbow, Summer, and a three-year-old toddler, the volunteering was about more than distributing goods. It was a lesson in empathy.

The father arrived on Wednesday night, when the fire broke out, with a different mission: to collect stuffed toys.
“Food, water, shelter – the government will take care of those things. I don’t worry about that,” he said. “But what do children really need? When they’re upset, they will not eat, and when they’re scared, they cannot sleep. What helps them sleep is a sense of safety.”
“A stuffed toy gives them emotional security. It tells them that someone cares.”
That night, he posted a message on Facebook and began collecting as many toys as he could carry.
“I came down here with six bags, all on foot,” said the father, who lives in nearby Fu Tip Estate.
Inside the shelter at Tung Cheong Street, he saw children lying on temporary beds.
“Some were asleep, but some could not fall asleep,” he recalled. “So I left two big bags of toys, told the staff, ‘For any child who cannot sleep, let them choose one. What they need is comfort,’ and then left.”

He worked until nearly 1am that first night. By Thursday, he had recruited his daughters. Before they left for school, he told them, “Do your homework after class. Then we’ll go help at Kwong Fuk Estate tonight.”
On Thursday, he met a parent and her two sons from the same primary school. Together, they reached the estate around 5pm and began handing out supplies.
Lam, a former concert director who lost his job during the pandemic and has battled depression, said that he hoped this experience would open his daughters’ eyes to the world.
“I asked them how they felt about volunteering,” he said. “I asked, ‘Are you happy because you’re playing, or because you’re helping people?’ They told me: ‘Because we’re helping.’ That really moved me.”
Reflecting on the mental health struggles many young people face today, Lam offered his own take on resilience.
“We hear too often about students taking their own lives … I think it’s because many kids today are fragile. Adults tell them not to look at sad things, not to feel pain, but I disagree,” he said. “We should teach children to face hardships, not run from them.”
“When something bad happens, we shouldn’t hide it. We should help them process it. I tell my daughters: if you feel sad, cry; if you feel uncomfortable, say it. But learn to face it.”
“You cannot just tell kids to be strong. You have to show them how,” he added. “That is why I brought my daughters here, to see suffering, understand it and learn how to comfort others. If they can do that, they’ll grow up knowing how to be kind and how to face life.”

data.title: Peru declares state of emergency as fears of migrant influx from Chile mount
data.description:
Peru’s government declared a state of emergency at its southern border with Chile on Friday, expecting an influx of people trying to flee far-right candidate Jose Antonio Kast’s potential presidency.
Under the decree, Peru’s military will reinforce border control in the southern Tacna region for 60 days, past Chile’s December 14 run-off election, in which immigration hardliner Kast is facing off against left-wing candidate Jeannette Jara.
The state of emergency will also serve “to address crime and other situations of violence” in the border area.
“The Peruvian National Police will maintain control of internal order, with support from the Armed Forces,” according to the order.
Peru’s President Jose Jeri announced earlier on Friday that such an action was forthcoming, after dozens of migrants were reported to be stuck at the Chile-Peru border.
A video posted by the Chilean governor of the Arica border region, around 2,200km (1,370 miles) north of Santiago, showed dozens of people attempting to exit from Chile at the Chacalluta-Santa Rosa border crossing.

A Venezuelan migrant told the online news outlet The Clinic that the group was trying to leave Chile “for fear that they remove us by force” if Kast were to become the next president.
“They don’t want to let us into Peru,” the migrant, who preferred to remain anonymous, said.
The Peruvian station Radio Tacna broadcast images of migrants carrying children on the highway near the border crossing.
Peru is both a source of, and country of transit for, migrants fleeing other parts of Latin America to Chile, one of the region’s most prosperous and stable nations.
The reverse migration trend comes just over two weeks before the Chilean presidential run-off, in which Kast is favoured to win.
Kast, a 59-year-old ex-MP on his third run for president, has given the country’s around 330,000 undocumented migrants an ultimatum to self-deport or be thrown out and lose everything if he takes office.

He blames undocumented migration for a surge in violent crime over the past decade.
“To undocumented immigrants in Chile, I say you have 103 days left to leave our country voluntarily,” Kast said in a video posted on Friday on his social media.
He was referring to March 11, the date when the successor of outgoing centre-left President Gabriel Boric is sworn in.
Peru’s Foreign Minister Hugo de Zela told a press conference Friday evening that the issue would be addressed via a “binational migration cooperation committee” beginning next week.
However, he stressed that Peru would not accept any more undocumented migrants.
Since 2015, more than 1.5 million Venezuelans have arrived in the country, fleeing a humanitarian and political crisis.
“We will not allow irregular migration. We do not have the conditions or capacity to receive more migrants,” he said.
